data.title: Amplify Energy Unlocks Substantial Value With Strategic Asset Sales
data.description:
Amplify Energy Corp. (NYSE:AMPY) has historically been an unfocused upstream oil and gas producer, previously owning five properties with no synergies between them, and with little control over cash flows (due to a majority of operations being from non-operated properties). After an oil pipeline leak in 2021, and a recent attempted merger at less-than-favorable terms for shareholders, the stock has sold off.

Major changes are underway after an activist investor, Clint Coghill, led a campaign to stop an unfavorable merger with Juniper Capital, and has now urged the company to change paths and take serious steps to unlock more value for investors. He shrunk the board from 8 to 5 people, invited in a new CEO with shareholder friendly performance compensation (awarded at $6-10 stock price), immediately sold one property, listed two non-operated properties more for sale, reduced SG&A and is focusing on the owned and operated "Beta" property that has attractive growth opportunities (with recently drilled wells far outperforming the expected yield curve). Clint is pursuing a sensible strategy, and as it has progressed he has been buying shares in the open market at $4. All things considered, I see 50%+ upside from here.

Note: All following DCF/PV figures are based on a perpetual flat oil price of $65, and a gas price of $4.

Background

Today, Amplify owns four properties located across the US:

Beta: An offshore oil field with 2 rigs and 1 processing center, in federal waters, 11 miles from Long Beach, Los Angeles, CA. It's 17,000 acres, 4.1 MBoe/d in production (June 2025 MTD production), 17 MMBOE proved reserves.

(All maps from Investor Presentation Q2 2025:)

Bairoil: This is a tertiary recovery project, a CO₂ flood oil field, in Wyoming. I consists of 7,000 acres, 3.1 MBoe/d in production, 12 MMBoe proved reserves.

East Texas/Northern Louisiana (ETX/NL): These are non-operated East Texas / North Louisiana Oil and Gas fields, covering 180,000 acres, 6.4 MBoe/d in production, 35 MMBoe proved reserves.

Oklahoma (OK): This is a non-operated Oil and Gas fields of 95,000 acres, 4.4 MBoe/d in production, 30 MMBoe in proved reserves.

Production data from the latest 10K 2024, allows us to value their assets properly:

Announced Strategic Sale of Two Major Assets

In July of 2025, Amplify chose a new CEO, Dan Furbee, who then launched a strategic review, sold one property for $23 million (Eagle Ford) and marketed the last two properties (East Texas/Northern Louisiana, and Oklahoma) for sale. The value of these properties alone is close to the enterprise value of the business.

Here some recent comparable sales of similar assets in the same basin.

Oklahoma has seen the sale of Conoco assets to Flywheel energy at $33.33 per flowing barrel equivalent, and Paloma sell assets to flywheel at $25.47 per flowing barrel equivalent.

In East Texas/ Louisiana, Aethon energy acquired Tellurian natural gas assets at $15.60 per flowing barrel, and Citadel Energy bought Paloma's natural gas assets at $17.14 per flowing barrel equivalent.

Based on these comps we should expect the OK assets to sell for around $29.40 per flowing (MBoe/d) and the ETX assets to sell for around $16.37 per flowing (MBoe/d).

That gets me to a value of $129 million for the OK assets and $104 million for the ETX/NL assets, or $233 million in total. Considering that AMPY has assets with significantly more higher liquid reserves and production, I think that selling at a premium to this is likely.

I can't see the inputs of managements PV-10 model, but in their investor materials they value their assets for sale using PV-10 at $185 million and $155 million in PV-10 at a fixed oil price of $65 and a fixed gas price of $4 (or $272 million all together, after a 20% haircut). When I recreate a PV-10 myself, I get to $189 and $198 million respectively (or $310 million with a 20% haircut).

My PV-10 model was based on the average 5% production decline, 6% production taxes, fixed LOE, a 10% discount rate, no new growth CapEX, just maintenance CapEX of $10 million and $12 million respectively.

This gives us a range of $233-310 million. For this write-up I will use the low end of that figure.

At AMPY's current enterprise value of $296 million, these properties (plus Eagle Ford) almost cover the current price of the business.

So what's left behind?

RemainCo is Attractive Following The Strategic Asset Sales

After this sale, Amplify is left with the Beta and Bairoil properties. Management values these together at $137 million (using PV-10), and the undeveloped fields at Beta at $133 million.

Bairoil doesn't have much upside. It is a tertiary recovery asset that can run for decades, albeit thinly. It will continue as-is and produce small amounts of cash.

Beta, however, has a few interesting points:

First, over 2024-2025, Amplify added 3 new wells that cost $17.8 million in capital to complete and based on PV-10 will yield $49.7 million in discounted cash flows, 179% return. This exceeds their expected yield curve based on production of 357 Boe/d per well and average drilling cost of $5-6 million.

They will be drilling 2 more wells in 2025, before pausing CapEX. Based on the yield curve, I expect these to add 700 boe/d to the operation by EOY.

Second, breakeven prices for new wells is $33/Bbl, due to minimal incremental costs and low Drilling and Completion Costs (offshore shallow water).

In early 2025, Amplify deferred $15 million of additional Beta drilling due to low commodity prices, which confirms that the 2024 CapEX program was skewed to growth projects.

The unit economics of drilling at today's oil price are good. When I model out 4 new wells a year (for 5 years, using up their remaining PUD), that cost $6 million each and yield 357 Boe/d. It increases the PV-10 value of the well by $300 million.

All of this is to say that I can see where the new CEO is coming from when he says "By simplifying the portfolio and focusing the Company on our Beta and Bairoil assets, I believe we can create a tremendous amount of value for all stakeholders.”

I get the following RemainCo cash flows and balance sheets, which clearly show how value will be unlocked.

Pro-forma Operating Cash Flows

Based on 8.3 MBoe/d production:

Current Balance Sheet

As reported (GAAP, 10-Q Q2 2025)

The asset sales should get us to the following pro-forma balance sheet:

Current Valuation is Attractive

At the current market cap value ($165 million), we find 50% upside from here (or 2x EV/FCF). As using conservative assumptions, you are getting $248 million in value for $165 million. Or, consider that post sale, the EV will be $54 million, and you are buying $26 million in pro-forma cash flows at baseline.

Heavy Insider Buying and Alignment

With a Newly Aligned Stock Comp Plan:

New stock based comp plan for the CEO has the following terms:

Fifty percent (50%) of the Target PRSUs shall vest if the 20-day volume-weighted average closing price (“VWAP”) of a share of Common Stock for the 20 consecutive trading days immediately preceding the end of the Performance Period equals at least $6.00 but less than $8.00, (ii) one hundred percent (100%) of the Target PRSUs shall vest if the 20-day VWAP of a share of Common Stock for the 20 consecutive trading days immediately preceding the end of the Performance Period equals at least $8.00, but less than $10.00, and (iii) two hundred percent (200%) of the Target PRSUs shall vest if the 20-day VWAP of a share of Common Stock for the 20 consecutive trading days immediately preceding the end of the Performance Period equals at least $10.00…The Performance Period is July 22, 2025 to March 31, 2028.

So management has 2.5 years to execute on this strategy.

Upside Optionality

There are positive changes in oil and gas prices, with global demand absorbing extra supply and the likely increase in energy needs and prices for various reasons, from AI to geopolitical conflicts. Carbon sequestration / CCUS credits also provide additional optionality for Bairoil.

Now that Amplify is as a pure oil company, it could be a M&A opportunity for one of the majors. Regarding the sale of Remaining Assets, Clint Coghill is very aligned with shareholders and focused on value creation. As we have seen with the Eagle Ford sale, historically he has sold productive assets at a good price. Once the company is even more subscale, it is very possible that Clint sells the remaining assets.

Further SG&A reductions are also tailwinds; as Clint Coghill stated in his activist letter to management, "Amplify needs to focus on cost structure optimization (noting that Amplify's G&A expenses of $35.9 million significantly exceeds the $11.7 million spent by SandRidge Energy, Inc., a company with a similar enterprise value and BOE/D production)."

Risks

Despite the previously mentioned positive direction that oil prices may go, there is always the risk of negative changes in oil and gas prices. Now that Amplify's commodity mix is 100% oil after the divestitures, this introduces a concentration risk where volatility can have an outsized impact.

There is also pipeline/environmental risk, which the company can mitigates. Its recent environmental incident stands as an example, when a souther California pipeline was struck by an anchor from a commercial ship. The resulting CapEX spending on the pipeline integrity upgrades was costly, as well a $96.5 million environmental settlement. But repairs should keep it safely functioning well from now on.

Key player risk specifically related activist investor Clint is something to watch. He built a business and sold it in 2021, and is now using this proceeds to invest. His net worth may be ~$10-20 million, so he does seem to have conviction here. However, he doesn't have direct experience in the O&G space. On the other hand, as an entrepreneur, he thinks like an owner with skin in the game. That's something that the company could benefit from quite a bit.

And lastly, there is execution risk on asset sales.

Catalysts:

Ten Oaks is running the sales process for the East Texas and Oklahoma assets, they state that bids are due for ETX and OK assets by September 4. Assets sales should close late Q4 2025 or early Q1 2026, and I expect a re-rate then. Management performance period is until March, 2028, so I expect them to continue to move fast.

Changes in the oil trip could materially affect future revenues in either direction. Also, as production from new Beta drilling locations comes online, cash flows realized, and Beta CapEX program restarted.

data.title: Prepare For Some Whipsaw In U.S. Treasuries
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas

We can see the 10yr Treasury yield targeting still lower as an attack on 4% is successful. But that's likely an overshoot to the downside. Higher inflation prints in the coming months will likely cause long-end yields some issues, requiring a significant backup. And we've seen this before. The last time the Fed cut, the 10yr yield rose (Sep-Dec 2024).

There is a path for a lower 10yr rate in the lead up to the first Fed rate cut, but that could be it

Following a week of poor labour market data, we're facing an inevitable rate cut on 17 September. The only debate is whether it will be 25bp or 50bp. We've been here before. In September 2024, the funds rate ceiling was at 5.5%, and it was cut by 50bp. Two more 25bp cuts followed in consecutive meetings, and then there was the pause (we're still on it).

One of the issues of contention from September to December 2024 was the rise in the 10yr yield as the Fed cut rates. There were material falls in the lead up to the first cut. But after that, the 10yr yield ratcheted higher, by some 120bp on aggregate. Hindsight shows there was an overshoot to the downside in the 10yr yield ahead of the first cut.

This is best encapsulated by the 10yr SOFR rate, which bottomed at a lowly 3.15%. That's quite a level when we consider it's not far from the bottom being discounting for the funds rate in the upcoming rate-cutting phase. Roll on then through December 2024, and that 10yr rate was heading to a new peak of 4.3% (or 4.8% for the 10yr Treasury yield).

We sense that something similar can occur in the coming months.

When the Fed cut last time, the 10yr rate commenced a ratchet higher process

The chart shows the 10yr and funds rate through the cut cycle from September to December 2024.

A rising inflation environment in the coming months is a reason for long rates to ultimately back up again

Why the 10yr yield rose from September through December 2024 is open to interpretation, but there was an underpinning of macro resilience, sticky-ish inflation, and lots of talk on fiscal largesse as a medium-term risk. This time around, granted, worries about the economy are more intense. But offsetting this are ongoing fiscal concerns and quite a different inflation dynamic.

When the Fed cut in September 2024, US inflation had eased to the 2.5% area, and had realistic ambitions to continue to edge lower. Fast forward to today, and US inflation is running at around 3%, and likely heading higher in the coming months on account of the tariff impact.

On top of that is the meddling with the Federal Reserve coming from the Trump administration, which is problematic when viewed through the prism of perhaps rates being cut by too much ahead. And the fiscal situation has not improved. Tariff revenue is underwhelming, tax cut costs are coming in 2026, and spending remains broadly unconstrained. These issues pose a risk for subsequent upward pressure on longer tenor yields.

Here's how the 10yr yield can get down to 3.75% and then back up to 4.5%

Currently the 10yr SOFR rate is 3.55%, and it is highly probable that US inflation trends up to the 3.5% area in the coming months. That implies a zero real rate, or the prospect of a negative real rate should the 10yr SOFR rate trend lower from here. Given the market mood, it likely will, partly as the inflation ahead has yet to be proven.

The absolute limit for the 10yr SOFR rate should be the bottom of the rate cut cycle, discounted now at 2.75-3.00%. Realistically, a 50bp spread to that is a fair value floor for the 10yr SOFR rate. That churns out 3.25% as an absolute low. And equates to a 3.75% 10yr Treasury yield (given the 50bp swap spread).

But these are not sustainable levels. It is quite probable that the market begins to run scared of upcoming inflation prints. We agree that the tariff effect is a higher price rather than a sustained higher inflation one. But that too still has to be proven.

If inflation prints in the 3.5% area, we'd suggest it's quite conceivable that the 10yr SOFR rate would have to find some comfort above this level. A backup to the 4% area is quite probable (or 4.5% for the 10yr Treasury yield).

This backs out a 100bp curve from the funds rate to 10yr SOFR or a 150bp curve to the 10yr Treasury yield as a call over the coming months. That's not aggressive for the curve. History shows that a 10yr term premium average of between 150-200bp is about right as a tendency to aim for.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: Aave Price Holds At $310 As Bulls Defend Key Support Zone
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

Aave (AAVE-USD) is currently trading at $310.25, recovering modestly after yesterday’s sell-off but still hovering below the $314–$318 resistance cluster. The token is consolidating just above the $309.5 20-day EMA and testing the 50-day EMA at $312.7, while the 200-day EMA at $315.7 forms the upper boundary of near-term resistance.

If AAVE manages a decisive close above $318, it could retest the $325–$330 zone, while failure to hold the $309–$310 base risks another slide toward the $300 psychological support. The RSI at 48.4 signals neutral momentum, with MACD still in negative territory, suggesting fading buyer conviction.

Technical and on-chain picture

On-chain flows highlight cautious behavior. CoinGlass data shows $2.28M in exchange inflows on September 5, signaling short-term distribution and profit-taking pressure. This contrasts with earlier outflow-led accumulation, suggesting whales remain divided - some reducing exposure while others continue rotating into cold storage. Market cap has stabilized around $4.5B, reflecting resilience compared to smaller DeFi peers despite recent volatility.

Technically, price action is in a make-or-break setup. A sustained close above the $318–$322 zone would signal the beginning of a fresh upward leg, targeting the $330–$332 supply band.

Failure here would lock AAVE inside a $300–$318 consolidation range, where downside breaks risk retesting $290 support. RSI remains neutral, but MACD’s negative slope shows buying conviction is weak, leaving AAVE vulnerable to another correction if buyers cannot reclaim control quickly.

Fundamental positioning and outlook

From a fundamentals standpoint, AAVE continues to reinforce its blue-chip DeFi status. The Ethereum (ETH-USD) beta trade rotation has helped altcoin inflows tied to ETH’s relative strength, while the Aptos deployment of Aave V3 marked its first non-EVM chain expansion, broadening adoption beyond Ethereum-native ecosystems. The Horizon initiative is also noteworthy, designed to onboard tokenized real-world assets and strengthen Aave’s role as a bridge between TradFi and DeFi.

Institutional engagement remains supportive, highlighted by integrations such as Kraken’s white-label lending partnership and liquidity engine adoption. However, risks persist in the near term.

Phishing-related concerns have created temporary unease within the retail community, while regulatory scrutiny of DeFi governance tokens continues to weigh on sentiment. Together, these headwinds temper Aave’s otherwise positive growth trajectory.

Short-term outlook

Looking forward, AAVE’s short-term structure will hinge on whether bulls can clear $318 with conviction. A close above that level opens upside targets at $325–$330, and potentially $340, should flows return.

Conversely, losing the $309–$310 pivot could extend the correction toward $300, where the psychological support will be tested. Below this, $290 is the next critical level to watch. Traders should also monitor Ethereum closely, as AAVE’s 0.82 correlation with ETH means ETH’s direction will likely dictate AAVE’s momentum in the coming sessions.

In earlier analysis, the $318 resistance zone was identified as the level AAVE must break to flip momentum bullish. Current price action continues to respect that resistance, confirming its importance as the defining level for short-term direction.

data.title: EUR/USD Breaks Channel As Rate Cut Bets Ramp Up... Potential 370 Pip Rally Incoming?
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

EUR/USD finally got the catalyst it needed to break the channel that price had been holding since the back end of July.

August 22 and September 1 both saw EUR/USD attempt to break beyond the channel, but the move was met with swift selling pressure. This led many to believe that a catalyst may be needed to inspire a breakout, as the pair saw a lot of choppy price action over the last month.

Jobs Report Weighs on the US Dollar

Non farm payrolls rose only about 22k while economists guessed near 75k. There were about 29k upward revisions for the last two months, yet even after adding them, the total still looks like a small miss.

Unemployment edged up to 4.3 percent from 4.2 percent, which was kind of expected, but underemployment, workers who want more hours, climbed faster to 8.1 percent from 7.9 percent.

Hours worked fell roughly to 34.2 per week, and wage growth slipped to 3.7 percent year over year instead of 3.9 percent, so softness appears everywhere.

Over the last two and a half years, almost nine‑tenths of new jobs came from just three fields: government, private health and education, plus leisure‑hospitality.

If you strip those three out, payroll numbers have actually slipped for four straight months, hinting at problems in sectors usually called growth engines of the U.S. economy.

As things stand, serious concerns are starting to be raised by consumers around the health of the US economy. Consumers expect the labor market situation to deteriorate rather than get any better.

According to recent University of Michigan data, 62% of Americans think unemployment will rise over the next 12 months, while only 13% think it will fall. This gives a net reading of 49% who expect unemployment to rise. We've only seen worse readings on four occasions in the past 50 or so years, as seen in the chart below.

Implications for the Fed

Market participants have, for the first time, begun pricing in the probability of a 50 bps rate cut in September. This has been one of the biggest reasons for the US dollar weakness we are seeing today.

According to LSEG data at the time of writing, markets were pricing in around an 11% probability of a 50 bps cut at the September meeting. This has in part led to a slide in the US dollar and seen gold prices attract haven bids.

The uncertainties and the potential for a recession are definitely rising, and this could also be why haven demand has remained strong.

The Fed's Beige Book released this week read grim, and that helped spark a 50 bp cut last year. Yet the current makeup of the Fed looks cautious, and the shadow of tariffs and inflation adds uncertainty. It is likely that a majority for another 50 bps cut won't form, though two or three members could vote for it.

Policy Divergence to Aid the Euro Through December 2025

The euro may also gain an advantage as policy divergence comes into play. Let me explain: the ECB may still cut rates once more this year, but that is about it.

The Fed is now scheduled for two rate cuts and potentially, if the job market worsens, three rate cuts this year, if not an early one in 2026. This sets the euro up handsomely to gain further ground against the greenback.

The Euro Area economy is grappling with its own challenges, though, so the narrative could change. For now, though, the outlook does favor further gains for the euro against the greenback.

Looking Ahead

Market participants will now turn their attention to a slew of high-impact data releases next week from the US.

PPI, CPI, and University of Michigan data will all be released and could have a further impact on rate cut expectations. A softer-than-expected CPI and PPI print could see the bets for a 50 bps rate cut in September rise and further bolster the euro.

Also keep an eye out for comments from Federal Reserve policymakers and how they receive and interpret today's data release.

Technical Analysis - EUR/USD

EUR/USD is breaking the channel on the four-hour chart, which has been in play since late July. We have seen two attempts to break the channel fail, and this time the NFP data may be the catalyst.

A four-hour candle close above the channel has occurred, and if the pattern for channel breaks is to conclude, it could result in a potential 370 pip upside rally. This could put EUR/USD around the 1.2100 mark should it come to fruition.

Now there are a host of fundamental factors discussed above that support the move. There are also a few that do not, so bear that in mind moving forward.

Looking at the period-14 RSI, it is currently in extremely overbought territory. This could lead to a short-term pullback before the bulls continue to move higher.

Looking at the four-hour chart, the swing low at 1.1631 will hold the key for me. A four-hour candle break and close below this level would have me reevaluating the pair. As long as this level remains support, the potential for a rally remains in play.

data.title: CrowdStrike Stock: Strong Key Metrics To Drive Stock Gains
data.description:
CrowdStrike (NASDAQ:CRWD) reported solid Q2 results primarily driven by strong demand for the company’s AI and cloud-native cybersecurity solutions. The company’s key metrics, particularly net new Annual Recurring Revenue (ARR), showed acceleration, which is quite impressive. The company’s net new ARR showed acceleration a quarter earlier than the management expectation, driven by strong AI demand. Management expects high single-digit sequential net new ARR growth in Q3 and over 40% YoY net new ARR growth in the second half of the year to be positive catalysts for the stock’s further upside.

In my previous article, I had highlighted that CrowdStrike’s single agent platform is driving the increased adoption of its modules by its customers, thereby contributing to an increase in ARR. Similarly, I had noted that Falcon Flex has been a game-changer as customers can adopt additional modules frictionlessly across the entire CrowdStrike platform. The successful adoption of additional modules is further leading to an increase in ARR, and re-flexes (follow-on deals) are leading to a 50% uplift in Flex customer ARR.

Net New ARR Acceleration

CrowdStrike’s Q2 ARR grew by 20% YoY to $4.66 billion. The company’s most important key metric, which is net new ARR, came at $221 million, up 1% YoY. It came ahead of management expectations by a stellar double-digit millions. Management has guided high single-digit sequential net new ARR growth in Q3. Using a conservative 7% sequential growth would imply 55% YoY net new ARR growth in Q3 to $236 million. The company is on track to achieve the $5 billion ARR by the end of this fiscal year and management reiterated its goal of reaching $10 billion ARR by the calendar year 2030.

The company’s net new ARR was previously negatively impacted by the Customer Commitment Package (CCP), which was introduced post the July 19, 2024 outage to rebuild customer confidence and ended in Q4 2024. The return to growth of net new ARR implies that the company has successfully navigated the issues arising from the July 19 outage.

The company is witnessing strong growth beyond its core endpoint security offerings. Emerging products like Cloud security, next-gen identity protection and next-gen security information and event management (SIEM) ARR grew by 44% YoY to $1.56 billion. Notably, the company’s AI-native platform Next-Gen SIEM ARR grew by 95% YoY to $430 million. The company also announced its plan to acquire Onum, a leading data pipeline platform. The deal is expected to facilitate faster onboarding for Next-Gen SIEM and robust agentic security operations with improved efficiency. It will also enable analysis even before data enters the Falcon platform, helping in early threat detection.

The company’s Falcon Flex has been very successful. CrowdStrike boosts over 1000 Falcon Flex customers with more than 100 signing re-flex deals within an average of five months from their initial flex subscriptions. Notably, a Fortune 500 software company signed an 8-figure re-flex deal with 18 months remaining on their initial flex contract to modernize their security operations center.

Strong Revenue Growth

CrowdStrike has experienced strong growth. It reflects the success of the company’s single lightweight Falcon platform and the proven land & expand strategy, wherein customers can easily adopt additional modules from the same Falcon platform. This easy integration also reduces the cost of ownership for the customers and, at the same time, increases the switching costs, thereby reducing customer churn. Q2 revenue grew by 21.3% YoY to $1.17 billion. Analysts expect Q3 revenue to grow 20.5% YoY to $1.22 billion and accelerate to 22.6% YoY growth to $1.30 billion for Q4.

Expanding Modules

The company’s customers are increasingly adopting more modules, taking advantage of the company’s single-agent platform. As seen in the chart below, the percentage of customers adopting more than 8 modules increased from 20% in the Oct 2024 quarter to 23% in the recent quarter. Customers with more than 6 modules have remained stable at 48% over the last three quarters and those with more than 7 modules have increased from 28% in the April 2024 quarter to 33% in the recent quarter. Notably, customers with over $100,000 in ARR have 60% of them adopting more than 8 modules, implying that larger customers are adopting more modules.

Margins

The company reported record adjusted operating income of $255 million in Q2, up 5.8% YoY. Adjusted operating margin came at 22%, above the management guidance of 20%. The margin outperformance was driven by operating leverage and cost-cutting initiatives. Management has guided the adjusted operating margin of 21% for Q3, implying a 100-basis-point improvement when compared to the same period last year.

The company has a stable balance sheet, with $4.97 billion in cash and $744.7 million in debt. The company reported record free cash flow of $283.6 million or 24% of revenue in Q2.

Risks

While the company reported strong Q2 results. The revenue guide for Q3 is in the range of $1.208 billion to $1.218 billion, which fell short of consensus estimates of $1.23 billion. This is a risk to watch as macroeconomic uncertainty could put pressure on corporate budgets and thereby negatively impact the company’s results.

The company is also facing intense competition from other cybersecurity companies like Palo Alto Networks, Inc. (PANW) and SentinelOne, Inc. (S), particularly after the July 19 software update that led to the outage. While CrowdStrike has managed to retain the trust of its clients, the company’s margins have been negatively impacted due to the pricing pressure from its competitors and the additional costs to maintain the relationships with its customers.

Valuation

The company is trading at a P/S ratio of 23.6x, below its average five-year P/S ratio of 29.8x. It is trading at a forward P/S ratio of 21.7x, below its average forward P/S ratio of 24.7x. The company has commanded a premium valuation due to its strong revenue growth potential; it was able to achieve GAAP profitability in the April 2023 quarter sooner than many other cloud companies. Although the company has temporarily slipped back to losses after the July 19, 2024, incident, it expects to be GAAP profitable again in Q4.

Conclusion

CrowdStrike is witnessing strong demand for its AI and cloud-native cybersecurity solutions. CrowdStrike’s single-agent Falcon platform facilitates easy scaling of additional modules, contributing to revenue and margin expansion. The company’s revenue and profits are expected to have strong growth in the coming years. The company reported strong Q2 results, with key metrics rising, particularly net new ARR, which accelerated a quarter ahead of management expectations.

data.title: ICLN: Favorable Rate Environment, AI Expansion Present An Attractive Buying Opportunity
data.description:
It would be a real surprise that a year into the Trump presidency we would be having a year of renewable energy outperformance, in terms of the iShares Global Clean Energy ETF (NASDAQ:ICLN), shelling out a pretty 25% YTD return.

I will be arguing that more returns are coming soon, with the upcoming Fed easing boosting earnings and the never-ending need for energy in the AI expansion.

The Fund

ICLN aims to provide exposure to the renewable energy trend, investing in solar wind and other renewable energy sources. The fund is diversified in terms of geographic exposure with the largest region being the US. It is meant to track the performance of the S&P Global Clean Energy Transition Index, which consists of around 100 companies with at least $300 million in market cap, $100 million in float adjusted market cap and $3 million median value traded over a six-month horizon. Companies must derive at least 25% of their revenue from clean energy businesses in order to be included in this ETF. According to iShares:

Constituents are weighted based on a product of the constituent’s float-adjusted market capitalization and exposure score, with weights of constituents capped based on their exposure score and liquidity weight. The Index Provider determines liquidity weight based on the relative six-month median daily value of security compared to that measure for the overall index. In addition, the cumulative weight of all stocks within the Underlying Index that have a weight greater than 4.5% cannot exceed 40%.

Given these constraints into consideration, we arrive at the conclusion that the fund is dispersed in terms of individual company allocation. The expense ratio is 0.39% and with the generally low bid/ ask percentage spread of 0.07% it earns a B- Seeking Alpha Expense Grade. Liquidity is high as the average daily share volume on a 3-month basis is 3.01 million shares, and the fund AUM is $1.56 billion, boasting an A Seeking Alpha Liquidity Grade.

Regarding allocation, the fund is tilted towards defensive stocks, specifically utilities, with around 55% of assets, and the rest going to Technology and Industrials.

As previously mentioned, the fund’s top geographical region is the US, with China coming in second and Brazil third.

Compared to peers in the industry, ICLN has the lowest expense ratio and the highest AUM.

Looking through the lens of the Seeking Alpha ETF Grades, ICLN is has either industry level or better grades in each of the categories, with sector best grades for Liquidity and above average grade for Momentum and Expenses.

Following through, the dividend yield is best-in-class in relation to peers, the dividend growth is 13.56% on a 5-year CAGR basis.

Regarding performance, when we take a look at the last cycle through the 3-year price performance, the whole group is negative, as it was hit hard by the monetary tightening cycle and the higher rate environment, globally. The picture is the same on a 5-year basis.

Summing up, the fund tracks an index which constraints a concentrated fund structure, the expense ratio is low relative to other renewables funds, liquidity and AUM is higher, and it provides a better dividend yield, while performance is somewhat comparative on a longer track record.

Rate Cuts Provide Breathing Space for Renewables, Utilities

The last rate hike cycle was particularly intensive for the sector, from a valuation aspect as rates are a major fact when discounting future cash flows and from an income statement perspective as higher rates mean higher borrowing costs, pressuring margins and earnings. Just to illustrate, below are the top 10 constituents of ICLN and their respective debt ratios.

A bleak picture in terms of debt ratios, but a fantastic opportunity when we take into account the debt costs falling in the next couple of years as the easing cycle takes its course. Most of the companies have more debt than equity, with some such as Bloom Energy having a 246% Debt to Equity ratio.

Moreover, the easing cycle has another channel through which it increases the attractiveness of this ETF. Seeing as utilities are somewhat considered an alternative to bonds, an interest rate cut makes bonds less attractive compared to utilities.

And regarding rate cuts, the probabilities for at least one rate cut are 48.9% and 47% for at least two rate cuts for the October Fed meeting.

In my opinion, the slowing of hiring as shown in the July jobs report, the previous revisions of the labor market data provide potential for more rate cuts than the market currently anticipates.

The PBOC has already set its course for monetary easing, announcing 10 new monetary measures in May this year, from initial rate cuts to lending expansion and capital market liquidity tool optimization.

AI and the Need for Energy Capacity

AI is expected to be a major driving force for renewable energy companies, impacting operational expenses by enabling preventive maintenance, efficiency as for example boosting solar energy production by 20% just by optimizing panel orientations. The renewable energy market, boosted by strong AI demand is expected to rise from $1.34 trillion in 2024 to $5.62 trillion in 2033. Linked to data centers and energy demand, the expected global energy demand is expected to grow from 1% in 2022 to over 3% by 2030. Goldman Sachs expects 40% of the extra power to be satisfied by renewables.

Valuation

From a valuation perspective the fund has a P/E ratio of 14.75x, trading at a discount compared to the market’s 27.16x, and the U.S. Energy sector’s 16.39x.

The top 10 constituents generally trade at a low P/E ratio and some are trading below their 5Y average.

The chart points to a positive momentum, as the current price rose above the short and long MAs.

Risks

Of course, the risks to my thesis are the opposite of my anticipated catalysts. Tariff impact could provide upside for inflation expectations, although the first anticipated effects are a one-off increase in the price of goods. A decrease in the projected data center capacity, which is projected to increase by 50% by 2027, could mean less energy demand and slowing earnings growth for the renewable energy companies.

Recent risks arise from regulations coming from the Trump administration and the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, as it highlights major changes coming to green energy tax credits. The bill includes transition provisions for solar and wind projects, increasing the phase-out while still maintaining the longer runway for areas such as storage, hydropower and geothermal. A more significant provision in the Act is the phase-out of the 30% tax credits for wind and solar, the bill provides a one year window for construction to start in order to be eligible for the tax credit since the enactment of the bill or the project be fully operational by the end of 2027. The previous regulation encompassing this area had the credits in place through 2032. According to energy researcher Rhodium, the law risks $236 billion of renewable energy projects and around $110 billion of accompanying manufacturing projects, while emphasizing the need for renewables in the battle for global AI leadership and the headwinds this bill brings it. And I agree, phasing out renewable energy when faced with increased demand is not sustainable especially when we take into account that the green transition is in full effect.

Conclusion

ICLN is a quality ETF providing exposure to the renewable energy revolution, representing in my opinion an adequate choice among the peer group with superior liquidity, expense ratio and fund dispersion. The fund trades at a discount both to the broader market and the U.S. Energy sector. Given tailwinds arising from monetary easing and further AI expansion boosting energy needs, I rate ICLN a BUY, while maintaining a vigilant stance towards effects arising from recent regulatory changes.

data.title: Wheels Up: Rebound Potential With New Memberships Unveiled
data.description:
As we exit the Q2 earnings season, it has become apparent that investors are pushing back against the high valuations in the stock market. In my view, this is actually a time to take a smart "risk-on" attitude by investing in lesser-known, underappreciated small- and mid-cap stocks. By positioning ourselves this way, we're trading a bit of valuation and momentum risk for fundamental risk, which I think is the right move to make when the S&P 500 is hovering above a >20x P/E with a huge concentration in large-cap tech stocks.

Wheels Up (NYSE:UP), in particular, is a stock that has quietly outperformed the S&P 500 this year, after narrowly dodging de-listing status at the New York Stock Exchange for briefly dipping below $1 per share. In reality, this private aviation company has signaled a path back to growth with a new membership plan, improving flight profitability, and healthy liquidity, thanks in part to its partnership with Delta Airlines (DAL). Up ~30% since January, I think Wheels Up still has further room to rally.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Wheels Up in June, when the stock was trading near $1.50 per share. Since then, Wheels Up has soared ~60%, but in my view, this is just the start of a longer rebound as the company unveils a new, simplified membership plan that still offers flexibility for different types of private travelers. Amid further evidence that the company is boosting its fleet utilization and stabilizing its loss profile, I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating on this stock.

The new Signature membership

Let's first discuss the most pertinent recent news on Wheels Up: its new simplified Signature Membership, which aims to draw in a new batch of pre-committed travelers after seeing a reduction in the company's charter members over the past few years.

The most significant feature of the Signature membership is the upfront commitment of $200,000. This works as a deposit and can be used against future flights aboard the company's fleet of Phenom 300 and Challenger 300 aircraft. But perhaps most importantly, there is a flexible mix-and-match available as well. This deposit can also be credited toward flights on Delta Airlines.

Members have a choice between two rate plans: dynamic and fixed access plans, continuing the customizable rate plans that prior members enjoyed.

Rates are shown above. Signature members who sign up for the Fixed plan get a tiered rate structure based on how much in credits they have deposited into their flight accounts. For example, a private flier with a $1 million balance can get an $8,695 hourly rate on the Phenom 300, which is a 6.5% savings against a person who has the minimum $200k balance.

A Dynamic member, meanwhile, is subject to variable pricing based on route demand. That being said, there are also larger incentives for depositing more funds into the plan. A $1 million balance, for example, gives 8% savings against the dynamic rate of the day.

To me, the refreshed Signature membership offers two key benefits for Wheels Up. First, it modernizes and simplifies the membership experience, allowing Wheels Up to go to market with a simpler sell and potentially land more members. Second, the incentive tiers on the deposit structure encourage wealthy members to deposit more cash, which potentially acts as significant, interest-free financing for Wheels Up as it looks to streamline operations and modernize its fleet.

Bookings stabilization and better fleet utilization

The new membership offer just launched in September, so we'll have to wait to see the impact on Wheels Up's financials. Meanwhile, however, there are already positive metrics that we can point to.

As shown in the chart above, Wheels Up has succeeded in stabilizing its demand, with gross bookings roughly flat at -1% y/y to $262 million in the most recent quarter. Most importantly, however, its Utility metric improved 10% y/y to 41.1 (which, as a reminder, measures the average hourly usage per aircraft within a month). The company's newer Phenom and Challenger aircraft, which are on offer in the new Signature memberships, are outperforming the older aircraft in Utility, at 49 hours and 54 hours per month, respectively.

Per CEO George Mathison's remarks in the Q2 shareholder letter:

The newer, premium Phenom and Challenger aircraft currently have the highest reliability in our controlled fleet. That, combined with the customer demand profile for these aircraft, is resulting in significantly higher Utility for our Phenom and Challenger aircraft versus our controlled fleet average, even in the early ramp-up phase of building these fleets. We expect further improvements in Utility as we scale the fleets and reduce the headwinds and inefficiencies that came from our conscious decision to accelerate paint and interior improvements into seasonally slower travel periods. Over 30% of our premium jets now feature our updated branded livery and interior, with more on the way. I am also pleased that we have continued to deliver solid operational performance as we navigate the complexity of our fleet transition. We expect our operational reliability to improve as we continue to transition the fleet.

Higher utility is also resulting in higher flight contribution margins (a measure of profitability that excludes corporate overhead and is useful for judging operational efficiency). Adjusted contribution margins saw 440 bps of y/y improvement, to 12.2%.

Of course, the key question that has been on investors' minds as Wheels Up executes its turnaround plan is, does the company have sufficient liquidity as it undergoes a gradual improvement in bookings and fleet utilization?

The good news is that Wheels Up has $107 million of cash on its books (though that's saddled by $422 million of debt), thanks to its financing partnership with Delta. This cash balance represents roughly ~1 year of adjusted EBITDA losses, based on the company's ~$85 million of negative adjusted EBITDA over the trailing 12 months.

But recent moves should improve liquidity and bolster Wheels Up's position to continue modernizing its fleet. In late August, the company divested some non-core services holdings to generate ~$20 million in additional liquidity (not included on the June balance sheet). We note as well that new Signature Members will bring at least $0.2 million in deposits at a minimum each, which should help to expand Wheels Up's cushion as well.

Risks and key takeaways

There are, of course, many risks that investors should consider before buying Wheels Up. Corporate travel is in a shaky spot as companies look to slash costs, which could reduce a key customer segment for Wheels Up (especially as the company doubles down on its strategy to pre-sell flight blocks to companies to boost utilization). We're also hoping the new Signature membership will spark renewed interest in the company and precipitate a return to bookings growth, which is far from certain.

That being said, with margins improving and a laser-focused management team that is keen to appeal to new members, I'd give Wheels Up the benefit of the doubt, especially when the rest of the stock market is expensive. Don't splurge on a large position here, but this is worth a small bet.

data.title: SOXL: Far More Risk Than Reward Since COVID Shock
data.description:
Direxion Daily Semiconductor Bull 3X Shares (NYSEARCA:SOXL) should have been one of the top ETF performers in recent years, with its ultra-leveraged design on one of the strongest rising sectors on Wall Street. However, the flaw and excuse for severe underperformance has to do with basic math and how compounding works.

When large drawdowns in price hit any investment security, the raw math in percentage returns to recover major losses becomes quite overwhelming in the real world. It doesn't matter if you own the hottest sector on Wall Street or something worse for total returns. The math is the math. Warren Buffett's Rule #1 in investing is to not lose money, because compounding in recovery mode can be similar to climbing a mountain without tools and gear.

SOXL provides a great example of why leveraged ETFs are usually best avoided in your portfolio. My view is that 3X products, either bull or bear, will eventually end up as regrets for wealth creation (if held over a number of years).

I have suggested readers avoid SOXL for a couple of years running. Since my last article in September 2024 here, this security has lost another -22.8% in value vs. respectable S&P 500 gains around +15% (including dividends).

And, believe it or not, despite a huge rally in semiconductor names since the 2020 COVID pandemic showed up, SOXL is sitting only marginally higher than it was six years ago. Three distinct downturns in the sector (including February-April 2020) have decimated this ETF's chance at compounding in line with simple semiconductor investments.

Let me explain my logic for a continued Sell/Avoid rating on the SOXL ETF for a 12-month outlook.

ETF Holdings

SOXL is the 3X bull product with a close but opposite performance relative to the Direxion Daily Semiconductor 3X Bear ETF (SOXS). Both aim to create 300% of the daily price change leverage on the NYSE Semiconductor Index (ICESEMIT), a rules-based, modified float-adjusted market capitalization-weighted index that tracks the performance of the 30 largest U.S.-listed semiconductor companies (according to SOXL's website description here).

The leverage for both is created by owning swaps with the largest NYC money center banks and brokerages as counterparties (too-big-to-fail institutions essentially). Swaps involve premiums and discounts (as the case may be in daily trading) that end up costing SOXL holders some capital each day. In addition, 0.75% annually is expensed to manage the fund, while some interest income is earned on sizable cash holdings (used as collateral for the swaps).

The top 10 holdings for SOXL are listed below, as of September 1st, 2025. The main individual company exposure (almost 30% of the index) is focused on Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), NVIDIA (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO), Qualcomm (QCOM), and Texas Instruments (TXN).

The website includes a discussion and disclosure that no guarantees are made that 3X daily performance will be achievable, while long-term gains/losses become less predictable as a function of the inherent leverage and costs over time.

Performance Review

If you owned SOXL during the low-volatility, rather steady increase in semiconductor stock values between January 2016 and January 2020 (just before the pandemic), you could have earned a spectacular gain, pushing +2,000% over four years, dramatically better than 1X (normal) ETF advances from the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) at +261% and plain vanilla Big Tech Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) at +140% for total returns including dividends.

Yet, this stretch proved very atypical for returns if you review semiconductor sector volatility and cyclicality. For example, since January 2020, SOXL has been a rather material underperformer vs. 1X designs. Over the past 68 months, a 3X buy-and-hold decision would have netted a minor +42% for your brokerage account. Conversely, SOXX at +205% and QQQ at +175% have earned dramatically more, employing significantly less investment risk.

That's not all the bad news for long-term holders of this leveraged-long ETF. Had you been unlucky enough to purchase your position at other cycle tops in the computer-chip sector, such as late 2021 and July of last year, your relative returns would have been even worse (in terms of lagging by a greater degree compressed over a shorter time span).

Your -64% loss on investment in SOXL from early January 2022 to the present was a disaster compared to the 1X products gaining +36% and +44%, respectively.

Almost the same relative returns over materially less time invested are evident in the snapshot below, taken from July 2024 to today. Over 14 months, -63% for a total return was a total disaster vs. the small -7% loss achieved by SOXX and +13% gain outlined by QQQ.

In summary, to achieve long-term rewards in this 3X leveraged product, steady gains are mathematically necessary, with limited drawdowns and volatility over time in the underlying semiconductor equities. Unfortunately, the stock market is known for its volatility, while major bear markets in the highly cyclical semiconductor industry historically hit every 2-3 years (measured from the early 1980s).

Final Thoughts

After failing to rally enough to recover the losses endured between July 2024 and April 2025, any downturn in the U.S. stock market could send SOXL back under $10 per share. This outcome would give nearly everyone purchasing this ETF over the past six years a sizable percentage loss on investment.

If the AI-hyped chip sector succumbs to a recession in GDP output, with falling orders, sales, and income levels, SOXL may still prove a security to avoid for another 6-12 months. I know such a scenario seems unlikely during the depths of a boom in chip-investor sentiment. However, cycle downturns do happen, often with little warning.

On the daily trading chart drawn below, you will notice how poorly overall SOXL performance has tracked vs. the S&P 500 index (lagging by -65%) over the latest 18-month period.

Price is trying to remain above its 200-day moving average, but any failure to do so may signal that another round of chip-sector selling is underway. I have also circled in gold the momentum peak for On Balance Volume reached in May. I would grade the current SOXL chart pattern and related momentum indicators as mixed, at best.

To me, the risks inherent in this leveraged-long ETF far outweigh potential rewards, especially near a Big Tech peak in pricing. I have been discussing the high odds of a technology bust in many of my articles over the past month. You can review my bearish take on ProShares UltraPro 3X QQQ (TQQQ) written several weeks ago here. In many respects, the same logic to avoid SOXL is equivalent to my bearishness on TQQQ.

I continue to rate SOXL a Sell. The smartest time to consider owning it is after a multi-month decline (at a minimum) in semiconductor names. After the super-strong run since April (rising over +200% into August's top), I cannot find a concrete reason to own it. You have to believe the Big Tech bubble will continue expanding to amazing heights as a foundational argument to be bullish. I am clearly not in this camp, having traded various investment booms/busts since 1986.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: Diageo: The Lesson That Can Be Learned From Tobacco Companies
data.description:
If you're a Diageo (NYSE:DEO) shareholder, chances are your investment is in the red, with shares back at 2012 levels and 50% below their all-time highs.

Why Investing In Diageo Hasn't Worked

Many of us read about the success legendary investors like Buffett and others made by holding staple companies like Coca-Cola (KO) for a long time, and we'd all love to replicate their winnings by investing in those super tangible businesses.

Sadly, for us, the majority of staple businesses today are very far from their glory days, when growth was a matter of geographic expansion and brand awareness. Diageo is no exception. As is, a company like Diageo lacks most of the characteristics of a market-beating investment. Indeed, Diageo and other staple giants have been major laggards over the past decade:

Diageo simply doesn't have the growth prospects because it is already anywhere and everywhere. It has also squeezed every bit of juice out of pricing power during the pandemic. Further, Diageo's expansive operations have limited room for efficiency and consistent margin gains.

And, while Diageo's inherent upside is limited, it's also facing multiple headwinds and risks that don't seem to go anywhere, including rising costs due to inflation and sharp declines in alcohol consumption.

On top of all that, I called out long ago that the high barriers to entry into the industry have sharply eased in the era of direct-to-consumer and outsourced manufacturing. Nowadays, it seems every celebrity has their alcohol brand.

So, Diageo has to share a smaller pie (or one that's barely growing) with more competitors, and it cannot offset its smaller piece with higher profits, given the limits on margin expansion.

That, my friends, is typically a clear stay-away situation as investors.

Valuation Took A Long Time To Catch Up

Generally speaking, I belong to the camp that believes that if a business is struggling, you simply shouldn't invest in it, regardless of valuation. That said, it certainly doesn't help that Diageo and many of its peers came to this undeniable down cycle with lofty valuations.

In late 2021, Diageo traded above 30 times earnings, and it wasn't until 2024 that shares finally dropped below the market average valuation.

The absence of growth, a struggling business, and a lofty valuation all converged to yield the poor results. That's all important to understand as we look for something that will change that going forward.

The Business Continued To Struggle In H1

Diageo is perceived by many investors as a safe and predictable business. I argued for a long time that investors will eventually stop assigning mid-20x multiples on Diageo and many of its peers, and now it finally happened. With such businesses, though, investors are always on the lookout for a sign of change. Unfortunately, I'm not seeing it.

Sales in fiscal 25, which ended in June, were down 0.1% on a reported basis and up 1.7% on an organic basis, which excludes FX impacts, divestitures, and acquisitions. Growth was driven by a 0.9% increase in organic volume and a 0.8% rise in price/mix.

EPS dropped 39% Y/Y, or 8.6%, on an adjusted basis, as a minor improvement in gross margin was more than offset by higher operating expenses.

Looking at historic organic growth across divisions, we can see that even in segments where Diageo grew, it came from the lapping of an even sharper decline in the previous period, meaning that, except for Africa, all regions are still below their F24 levels.

Looking ahead to fiscal 2026, Diageo is expecting another year of flat sales on an organic basis (which likely means a decline in reported sales). Organic profit is expected to grow mid-single-digit, but that will come in the back half of the year.

On free cash flow, it expects a $250 million improvement, which reflects high-single-digit growth, but that is almost entirely due to lower capex.

All in all, the worst is maybe over, but that doesn't mean that what's ahead is good enough. I believe that tariffs are a new and valid excuse, but I don't think investors should just overlook them. At the end of the day, the most likely outcome for next year is no growth and flat profits, which is in line with current consensus estimates.

Valuation & The Tobacco Lesson

Going back to what I said before, I encourage investors to avoid investing in struggling businesses, regardless of valuation. Sometimes I get the response that I should look at how well tobacco companies performed when everybody said they were dying businesses.

Indeed, I think that there are some similarities to draw between the tobacco and alcohol industries, specifically that both aren't growth industries and that they are viewed in some way as "sins."

However, there are several vital nuances. First, tobacco is a consolidated industry, where competition is only declining. Second, the leading tobacco companies developed new products that are growing rapidly. Three, all tobacco companies traded at extremely depressed valuations before their rallies.

In fact, some of them still trade far below Diageo levels, and all of them traded below where Diageo is today until late 2024. This goes to show that Diageo is certainly not trading at depressed valuations just yet, and it has much less to lean on going forward.

In my mind, although I don't see it happening soon, I believe Diageo is a business that should trade at low-teens multiples, at best.

Conclusion

Even after a 50% fall from all-time highs, Diageo shares still trade at a valuation that doesn't reflect its lack of growth prospects, profitability woes, and increasing competition.

The alcohol industry, although it's viewed by some as comparable to tobacco, has very critical differences that make it far less attractive for investment.

As Diageo's business shows no sign of improvement, I argue investors should allocate their money elsewhere. That said, I believe the downside is somewhat limited.
